betterquesting:10.desc:8:
§6Boron§f

§aBluish-grey neutron-absorbing metalloid — essential for reactor control and the ZIRNOX!§f

§bBoron§f (atomic number 5) is a rare bluish-grey metalloid. Its atomic properties make it useful as a neutron absorber.

§6Production§f:
§b• Rare Earth Ore§f — process in anvil (Steel tier+) for Boron + Lanthanium + Cobalt + Cerium + Neodymium + Niobium fragments
§b• Coal Ash§f — centrifuge for Tiny Pile of Coil, Boron, Dust
§b• Rare Earth Chunks§f — centrifuge for Cobalt + Boron + Niobium + Zirconium
§b• Borax§f — acidize with Sulfuric Acid (more efficient) or arc furnace
§b• Red Mud / Gravel§f — process in SILEX
§b• Electrolysis§f — rare earth crystals + Nitric Acid = molten boron (larger quantities)
§b• Cyclotron§f — beryllium + lithium or vice versa

§6Key Uses§f:
§b• RBMK Neutron Absorbers§f — 8 Boron Ingots around Structural Column
§b• RBMK Console§f + §bCrane Console§f
§b• ZIRNOX Nuclear Reactor§f — requires 8 Boron Ingots
§b• Chicago Pile Control Rods§f
§b• PWR Control Rods§f
§b• Cyclotron recipes§f — make Aluminium and Coal powders
§b• Boron Glass§f — RBMK glass cover panels and laboratory glassware
§b• Desh Blend§f component
§b• Liquidator Medal§f

§6Boron Sand§f: Craft from boron powder + sand, smelt into boron glass.

§eTip§f: Boron is §erelatively abundant§f as a byproduct. Don't go out of your way to mine it — just centrifuge your coal ash and rare earth chunks!
